content.full_text:
NOV 26 '91 17:12    CA.ST.TREAS.TRUST SLP    963 F02




                                11-26-91

                        STATE TREASURER'S GUIDE
                       CONFLICT OF INTEREST RULES
                           IN A NUTSHELL

     It has been said the beginning of wisdom is to know there
are things we do not know. In that spirit, this brochure is
intended to help government officials recognize when their
conduct in a public finance transaction might be impacted by
the Political Reform Act.  State Treasurer Kathleen Brown
has prepared this brochure with the hope that it will
educate and inform public officials and private contractors
about the Political Reform Act and will help maintain the
high degree of integrity which is expected for public
finance transactions.

     There is no free lunch.  The dinners, briefcases, theater
and ball game tickets, and other perks given to public
officials by underwriters in public finance transactions
raise issues under the Political Reform Act, even when
underwriters are reimbursed by public officials from bond
proceeds for these "expenses".  This "wining and dining"
will subject officials to the various requirements of the
Political Reform Act.  Violation of these requirements may
lead to civil and criminal penalties.

     Recent publicity suggests that some government officials and
investment bankers are unclear about the Political Reform
Act rules as they apply to a public finance transaction.
This brochure outlines the significant events that may
trigger a requirement, limitation, or prohibition under the
Political Reform Act.  This brochure should not be viewed as
a substitute for directly consulting the law and counsel to
discuss the applicability of the Political Reform Act to any
particular situation.

     BASICS

     A state or local public official may not make, participate
in, or attempt to influence a government decision in which
he or she has a financial interest.

     The fundamental purpose of the Political Reform Act is to
ensure that officials perform their duties in an impartial
manner.  To achieve this goal, officials must understand
when a financial interest - through gifts, income, or
investments - may lead to a conflict of interest or the
appearance of a conflict of interest.  While gifts may not

appear to fall into this category, they constitute a form of
income. Accepting a gift can create a financial interest.

There are limitations on the value of gifts which may be
accepted, there are requirements that certain gifts be
publicly disclosed and there are restrictions on how bond
proceeds may be spent. This brochure will outline these
requirements.

A convenient way to remember the more frequently encountered
requirements and limitations is in association with the
minimum dollar thresholds -- $50, $250, and $1,000 -- for
disclosure, disqualification, and prohibited gifts.

1. DISCLOSURE

Most elected officials and government officers covered by
conflict of interest codes are required to file statements
of economic interests. These statements must be filed upon
assuming office, annually thereafter, and when leaving
office. These statements generally require disclosure of
management positions, investments, income and gifts. When
an official receives gifts aggregating $50 or more or other
sources of income totaling $250 or more from a single source
(which may be a person, corporation or other legal entity)
in a reporting period, the source of the gift or income
must be disclosed in the statements of economic interests.
Public officials should consult their conflict of interest
code and agency counsel to understand the requirements and
disclosure categories applicable to their activity.

2. DISQUALIFICATION

An official must disqualify himself or herself from a
government decision in which they have a financial interest.
A financial interest in a decision exists if the decision
will have a material effect on the official or a member of
the official's family.

Disqualification from the decision-making process includes
refraining from: (1) actually making a decision, (2)
participating in a decision, and (3) attempting to influence
a decision. The effect of a decision is material if the
decision will result in the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of the official, or his or her
immediate family, increasing or decreasing by at least $250.

3. PROHIBITED HONORARIA AND GIFTS

Generally, the Political Reform Act does not prevent
officials from owning or acquiring a financial interest
which may be in conflict with their official duties, nor
does it require officials to resign when such interests are
acquired. However, effective June 5, 1990, local elected

NOV 28 '91 17:13    CA.ST.TREA.TRUST SLR    982 P04

officeholders may not accept any honoraria or gifts from any
single source in excess of $1,000 in any calendar year.
Certain travel and subsistence expenses, however, are
permissible.    The FPPC is in the process of adopting rules
to define what is reasonable in this area.

PROHIBITED CAMPAIGN CONTRIBUTIONS

Most officials are prohibited from accepting, soliciting or
directing a campaign contribution of more than $250 from any
party with a financial interest in a contract involved in a
finance transaction which is pending before the official's
agency.  Local officials directly elected over voters are
excluded from this prohibition only when acting in their
elected official capacity. The prohibition applies when the
elected official is acting from an appointed position (not
based on the elected office) such as a director of a
nonprofit corporation, member of a transit board, or a
member of a joint powers authority.

ITEMS WHICH ARE NOT GIFTS

Most of the inadvertent violations of the Political Reform
Act in public finance transactions have involved the failure
of public officials to report and/or disqualify themselves
from participating in a government decision because of the
receipt of gifts.  Therefore, it is helpful to know the
major exclusions from the definition of the term "gift".
Gifts do not include:

--gifts which are reimbursed to the donor by the recipient
within 30 days.

--campaign contributions;

--informational material such as books, reports, pamphlets,
calendars or periodicals;

--gifts from certain close relatives;

--inheritance;

--gifts which are not used and returned within 30 days to
the donor or delivered to a charitable organization
without being claimed as a donation for tax purposes;

--personalized plaques and trophies with a value less than
$250.

TRAVEL AND RELATED EXPENSES PAID BY UNDERWRITERS

While many officials are sensitive to the issues involving
normal "wining and dining", a recurring problem involves the

NOV 26 '91 17:14    CA.ST.TREA.TRUST SUR         363 P05


payment of travel and per diem expenses for both elected and
non-elected government officials.  These expenses are
routinely paid by investment bankers to allow these
officials to attend rating agency presentations and other
bond related activities.  Normally, these expenses will be
reimbursed to the underwriter out of the proceeds of the
bond sale.  Under FPPC rules, however, if the reimbursement
does not occur within 90 days of the time the expenses were
incurred, the expenses will be deemed a gift to the
officials.

Travel and per diem expenses for an out-of-state trip are
typically in excess of the disclosure and disqualification
limits.  It will often be impossible to reimburse these
expenses from bond proceeds within 30 days of the time the
expenses are incurred.  Public officials would be required
to disclose source and size of the "gifts" (the expenses
not reimbursed within 30 days) and disqualify themselves
(e.g., signing the bond purchase contract, trust indentures,
or other financing documents, and decisions involving
selection of the underwriters to participate in the
transaction, and other acts involved in authorizing the
issuance of the bonds).

To avoid compromising the decision-making ability of public
officials, the FPPC has identified two methods public
agencies can follow: (1) the issuing public agency directly
pays the costs of attending rating agency meetings or other
bond related activities, or (2) the issuing agency follows
specific procedures that result in gifts being made to the
public agency rather than to the individual officials. The
latter approach requires:

     a) payments for travel be made directly to the public
agency and not a particular official;

     b) the public agency exercise substantial control over
who will travel and what expenses are incurred; and

     c) before the travel is arranged, the public agency
must formalize the making and use of the gift in a
resolution.

The Treasurer recommends that local agencies that issue
bonds prepare a general policy for dealing with the expenses
associated with a bond sale.  The State Treasurer's Office
will assist any agency in developing such a policy.

RECOMMENDED READING

The Office of the Attorney General has published a
comprehensive book on the Political Reform Act and other
conflict of interest rules applicable to state and local

NOV 26 '91 17:15     CA.ST.TREA.TRUST SUR     963 P06


government officials. The book is entitled Conflicts of
Interests. Copies of this book are available from the
Office of the Attorney General, P.O. Box 944255, Sacramento,
California 94244-2550.

CONCLUSION

The basic rules of the Political Reform Act are intended to
be straightforward. Many officials have stumbled because
they were not aware that the rules might apply to a
particular condition. Public officials and public contractors must
be especially vigilant in public finance transactions to
avoid violating the Political Reform Act. The integrity of
the public finance process must be above reproach.
Inadvertent violations may arise when officials reasonably
believe that reimbursement of certain expenses from bond
proceeds address the requirements of the Political Reform
Act. As pointed out in this brochure, public officials need
to follow specific procedures to avoid such violations.

Many officials have stumbled because they were not aware that the rules might apply to a particular conduct. Public officials and contractors must be especially vigilant in public finance transactions to avoid violating the Political Reform Act. The integrity of the public finance process must be above reproach. Inadvertent violations may arise when officials reasonably believe that reimbursement of certain expenses from bond proceeds address the requirements of the Political Reform Act. As pointed out in this brochure, public officials need to follow specific procedures to avoid such violations.
